title: Derivative as slope of curve
description: Practice your intuitive understanding of the derivative at a point as the slope of the curve (or of the tangent to the curve) at that point.
topic_path: Differentiation: definition and basic derivative rules
Defining average and instantaneous rates of change at a point

hints:
The derivative of a function at a point can be viewed as the "slope" of the function's graph at that point.

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7af076f9d15af466a64925b5b9b4b485aa8eab76)

Notice:

- The slope is positive.

- The slope is steeper than $1$.
The only reasonable estimate of $f'(5)$ is $2$.
The derivative $f'(x)$ of a function $(f)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $f$ at the point where $x=0$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/377e5a7e11ee1201478a6a41b03d69427f78f085)
We can see that the graph is constant at $x=0$, which means the slope must be equal to $0$.
In conclusion, $f'(0)=0$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the point where $x=2$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4e6bdc2c06b7b45eff502fbd0bf26847b7c380ea)

We can see that the graph is increasing at $x=2$, which means the slope must be greater than $0$.

Furthermore, we see that the slope is steeper than a slope of $1$.

The only appropriate value for $g'(2)$ out of our options is $3$.

In conclusion, $g'(2)=3$.
The derivative $h'(x)$ of a function $(h)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $h$ at the point where $x=-1$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3ed4753c719b706517995fdac04b198648723c43)
We can see that the graph is constant at $x=-1$, which means the slope must be equal to $0$.
In conclusion, $h'(-1)=0$.
The derivative $f'(x)$ of a function $(f)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $f$ at the point where $x=3$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/988689b6822bb0b1a3d76cafa1b35704c8331ba1)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at $x=3$, which means the slope must be less than $0$.

Furthermore, we see that the slope is more gradual than a slope of $-1$.

The only appropriate value for $f'(3)$ out of our options is $-\dfrac13$.

In conclusion, $f'(3)=-\dfrac{1}{3}$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the point where $x=4$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/cd57fb766c3fc8b0c40f602ef87c888a38579c3a)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at $x=4$, which means the slope must be less than $0$.

Furthermore, we see that the slope is steeper than a slope of $-1$.

The only appropriate value for $g'(4)$ out of our options is $-3.2$.

In conclusion, $g'(4)=-3.2$.
The derivative $h'(x)$ of a function $(h)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $h$ at the point where $x=10$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a84222e636a3e08c43be019e98f4263f777faf71)

We can see that the graph is increasing at $x=10$, which means the slope must be greater than $0$.

Furthermore, we see that the slope is more gradual than a slope of $1$.

The only appropriate value for $h'(10)$ out of our options is $0.5$.

In conclusion, $h'(10)=0.5$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the point where $x=1$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f55400d21fdd46253c4f4faf6e953100fcf63842)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at $x=1$, which means the slope must be less than $0$.

Furthermore, we see that the slope is steeper than a slope of $-1$.

The only appropriate value for $g'(1)$ out of our options is $-2$.

In conclusion, $g'(1)=-2$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the points where $x=4$ and $x=6$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b6f45d84c11d1c398ddf99f5bdb64eabb137117f)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at both points. This means both $g'(4)$ and $g'(6)$ are negative.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=6$. This means $g'(6)$ has a greater absolute value than $g'(4)$.

Since $g'(6)$ and $g'(4)$ are both negative, $g'(6)<g'(4)$.
In conclusion, $g'(4)>g'(6)$.
The derivative $f'(x)$ of a function $(f)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $f$ at the points where $x=-4$ and $x=-1$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/492cb64953fd28849850dcfcc59a6c116a48534f)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at both points. This means both $f'(-4)$ and $f'(-1)$ are negative.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=-4$. This means $f'(-4)$ has a greater absolute value than $f'(-1)$.

Since $f'(-4)$ and $f'(-1)$ are both negative, $f'(-4)<f'(-1)$.
In conclusion, $f'(-4)<f'(-1)$.
The derivative $h'(x)$ of a function $(h)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $h$ at the points where $x=-8$ and $x=-3$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e2b94d943c752968286d845b3521387a5ffb1274)

We can see that the graph is increasing at both points. This means both $h'(-8)$ and $h'(-3)$ are positive.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=-8$. This means $h'(-8)$ has a greater absolute value than $h'(-3)$.

Since $h'(-8)$ and $h'(-3)$ are both positive, $h'(-8)>h'(-3)$.
In conclusion, $h'(-8)>h'(-3)$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the points where $x=1$ and $x=3$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/736722a6a52515155486c6f83825d7be084435ee)

We can see that the graph is increasing at both points. This means both $g'(1)$ and $g'(3)$ are positive.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=3$. This means $g'(3)$ has a greater absolute value than $g'(1)$.

Since $g'(1)$ and $g'(3)$ are both positive, $g'(3)>g'(1)$.
In conclusion, $g'(1)<g'(3)$.
The derivative $f'(x)$ of a function $(f)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $f$ at the points where $x=-4$ and $x=-1$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/fed1950fe14130c386f7fe71c7efb26214566f07)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at both points. This means both $f'(-4)$ and $f'(-1)$ are negative.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=-1$. This means $f'(-1)$ has a greater absolute value than $f'(-4)$.

Since $f'(-4)$ and $f'(-1)$ are both negative, $f'(-1)<f'(-4)$.
In conclusion, $f'(-4)>f'(-1)$.
The derivative $h'(x)$ of a function $(h)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $h$ at the points where $x=-1$ and $x=2$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b7270dd1c2db7e1b0d7458ddfc083927f4bb4e94)

We can see that the graph is increasing at both points. This means both $h'(-1)$ and $h'(2)$ are positive.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=2$. This means $h'(2)$ has a greater absolute value than $h'(-1)$.

Since $h'(-1)$ and $h'(2)$ are both positive, $h'(2)>h'(-1)$.
In conclusion, $h'(-1)<h'(2)$.
The derivative $g'(x)$ of a function $(g)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $g$ at the points where $x=1$ and $x=8$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/5c8c2101e99d546f1744c48c035ec368b1928f32)

We can see that the graph is decreasing at both points. This means both $g'(1)$ and $g'(8)$ are negative.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=1$. This means $g'(1)$ has a greater absolute value than $g'(8)$.

Since $g'(1)$ and $g'(8)$ are both negative, $g'(1)<g'(8)$.
In conclusion, $g'(1)<g'(8)$.
The derivative $h'(x)$ of a function $(h)$ at a point $(x)$ can be viewed as the steepness and direction, or slope, of the function's graph at that point.

Here we see the steepness and direction of the graph of $h$ at the points where $x=1$ and $x=6$:

![graph](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f9b8c22102b0f1e0980842f034aefa548e029290)

We can see that the graph is increasing at both points. This means both $h'(1)$ and $h'(6)$ are positive.

Furthermore, we can see the graph is steeper when $x=1$. This means $h'(1)$ has a greater absolute value than $h'(6)$.

Since $h'(1)$ and $h'(6)$ are both positive, $h'(1)>h'(6)$.
In conclusion, $h'(1)>h'(6)$.
